skill.name: writing-data
skill.description: Use this skill when you need to structure data in `srs/data` for the Next.js app
skill.summary: Use this skill when you need to structure data in `srs/data` for the Next.js app
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: The static analyzer flagged backtick usage and a weak cryptography pattern, but the file only uses markdown inline code for the srs/data path. No shell execution, cryptographic code, network access, data exfiltration, or prompt injection text was found in SKILL.md.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Backticks in Description
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at SKILL.md line 3. The line is YAML frontmatter text using markdown inline code for the srs/data path, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is markdown with YAML frontmatter, and the backticks surround a directory path. There is no Ruby, shell script, command substitution, or execution context.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Backticks in Instruction Text
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported Ruby or shell backtick execution at SKILL.md line 8. The line is a bullet point that names the srs/data directory using markdown inline code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious token appears only in prose inside a markdown instruction. No external command invocation, interpolation, or user-controlled execution path exists.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at SKILL.md line 3. The reviewed line contains only the skill description and a directory path reference, with no hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API usage.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line contains no crypto terms, imports, algorithms, or executable code. The finding appears to be a pattern collision against ordinary documentation text.

content.value_statement: Next.js data files can become hard to maintain when content grows. This skill guides modular data organization in srs/data with one item per file.
actual_capabilities: Guides data organization for a Next.js app directory named srs/data.
Recommends modular files instead of one large shared data file.
Applies a maximum of 50 lines of code per data file.
Encourages one data item per file for clearer ownership.
Supports splitting data into multiple files when that improves maintainability.
limitations: Does not provide a schema, validator, or code generation script.
Does not define TypeScript types or runtime validation rules.
Uses a narrow directory convention that may need adjustment for other projects.
Does not explain import, export, or naming conventions in detail.

use_cases.title: Organize App Content
use_cases.description: Break growing content data into small files that are easier to review and update.

use_cases.title: Prepare Feature Data
use_cases.description: Create a clean file layout before adding new static data to a Next.js feature.

use_cases.title: Review Data Structure
use_cases.description: Check whether existing data files follow modular size and single-item organization rules.

prompt_templates.title: Organize One Data Item
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single new data entry.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-data skill to place this new data item in srs/data. Keep it modular and use one data item per file.

prompt_templates.title: Split a Large File
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request to improve an existing file.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-data skill to review this data file and suggest a split plan. Keep each resulting file under 50 lines.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Feature Data Layout
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate request before adding a new feature.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-data skill to design a modular srs/data layout for this Next.js feature. Separate data into multiple files when possible.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Data Organization
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the writing-data skill to audit the srs/data folder. Identify files that violate the one data per file rule or exceed 50 lines.

output_examples.input: A single file contains several testimonial records for a Next.js page.
output_examples.output: The skill would recommend separate testimonial files, each focused on one record, with an index or aggregator only if the project needs it.

output_examples.input: A new feature needs static card data for several sections.
output: Create one small data file for each section.
Place the files under the project data directory convention.

output_examples.input: A code review asks whether the current data folder follows the project rules.
output_examples.output: The skill would flag oversized files, mixed data concerns, and files that combine multiple independent data items.

best_practices: Keep each data file focused on one clear data item.
Split files before they exceed the 50 line guideline.
Use consistent names so related data files are easy to find.
anti_patterns: Do not combine unrelated data items into one large file.
Do not keep adding entries after a file becomes hard to scan.
Do not use this skill as a replacement for validation or type design.

faq.question: What does this skill help organize?
faq.answer: It helps organize data files for a Next.js app using the srs/data directory convention.

faq.question: Does it generate working application code?
faq.answer: No. It provides structure guidance for data files, not complete application logic.

faq.question: Can it review existing data files?
faq.answer: Yes. It can check whether files are modular, small, and limited to one data item.

faq.question: Does every file need to stay under 50 lines?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill states a maximum of 50 lines of code per file.

faq.question: Is this limited to Next.js projects?
faq.answer: The instructions are written for a Next.js app, but the modular file principles may help similar projects.

faq.question: Does it define data schemas?
faq.answer: No. You should add separate schemas, types, or validators when the project needs stronger guarantees.
